schools.summary: Herod Agrippa I (A.D. 44, the year of his death), grandson of Herod the Great, brother of Herodias; the civil power now turned against the church.
Chrysostom: rest and trouble alternate in the text — God blends them.
Calvin: the church had a short truce to recover courage and fight afresh; not to think one or two conflicts the end.

James, son of Zebedee, the 'son of thunder,' first apostle to die — fulfilling Christ's prophecy of his cup (Mark 10:38); beheading by the sword marked a civil, Roman-style sentence, not Jewish stoning; not James the Lord's brother.
Herod seized Peter to please the Jews — persecution from political expediency, not fanatic zeal (the Herodian policy); the days of unleavened bread deferred the public execution.
Calvin: Herod persecuted to provide for his own affairs and win the people's favour, not from zeal for the law.

Four quaternions = 16 soldiers, four per watch, Peter chained between two — his third imprisonment; 'after Easter' an absurd mistranslation of 'after the Passover' (meta to pascha); escape humanly impossible.
Calvin: Peter shut up as in his grave, quite past hope — the extremity in which God acts.
'Prayer made earnestly' (ektenos) — fervent, intent, persevering; the church meeting as usual at Mary's house; the deliverance a signal answer to prayer.
Calvin: all the church fought with their prayers alongside the imprisoned apostle — the church's weapon against the king's power.
The night before execution Peter sleeps calmly, chained between two soldiers — the sleep God gives his beloved (Ps 127:2), undisturbed by fear of death.
Henry: a peaceful conscience, lively hope, and the Spirit's comforts keep men calm in the prospect of death.

The angel 'stood by him' (epeste, as to the shepherds, Luke 2:9), light in the cell, a touch to wake him, and the chains fell off — the angelic ministry that had freed the apostles before (Acts 5:19).
Calvin: perhaps Peter alone saw the light, the soldiers amazed or in deep sleep, God blinding their senses.

'Gird thyself, bind on thy sandals, follow me' — Peter dresses fully and unhurriedly (Clarke: a proof all was done leisurely, no hurry); the rescue proceeds with calm order, owing nothing to human haste.
Peter 'wist not' it was real, kept following, 'thought he saw a vision' — the dreamlike daze of a deliverance beyond hope; the introspective detail suggesting Peter as Luke's informant (Ellicott).
Past the guard-posts to the iron gate, which 'opened of its own accord' untouched; the angel led him one street then departed — every obstacle yielding without human effort.
Calvin: God overcame the straits one after another, that the glory of the miracle might be the greater.

themes: the iron gate opened of itself

schools.summary: 'Come to himself' (as the prodigal); the daze gone, he knows 'of a surety' the Lord sent his angel and delivered him from Herod — a personal reminiscence, ascribing all to God.
Calvin: his words a thanksgiving, extolling the benefit of God.
Mary's house, home of John Mark (the evangelist, Peter's 'son' 1 Pet 5:13, cousin of Barnabas); 'many gathered praying' — the church interceding for the very deliverance now accomplished.
Calvin: Mary a matron of rare godliness, her house as a temple of God; Henry: they continued in prayer, not fainting.

themes: the freed apostle seeks the church

schools.summary: Peter knocks at the gateway (pylon); 'Rhoda' (Greek, 'rose'), a servant girl, comes to listen — Luke's naming of the slave shows his care for detail; the door shut for fear of the Jews.
Knowing Peter's familiar voice, Rhoda 'for joy' (apo tes charas) forgets to open the gate and runs to tell — gladness overpowering presence of mind, a life-like touch characteristic of Luke.

'Thou art mad' — a curious rebuff from those praying all night for his release; they would not believe the answer, supposing 'his angel' (the Jewish guardian-angel belief, on which Scripture is silent) — God answers more readily than his people believe.

Peter 'continued knocking' until all heard; opening, they 'saw him' and were astonished — their amazement the measure of how little they expected the answer; his persistence urgent, in danger of recapture.

Beckoning for silence (kataseisas), he tells 'how the Lord brought him out' (crediting God), sends word to James (the Lord's brother, leader of the Jerusalem church) and the brethren, then withdraws — grateful and prudent.
At daybreak 'no small stir' (litotes) — the sixteen soldiers in confusion and dread at Peter's inexplicable disappearance, knowing escape meant their death under Roman law.
Chrysostom grapples with why the guards suffered — the time of judgment, when each is rendered his due, was not yet.

Herod examines (anakrinas, forensic) the keepers and has them executed — the Roman penalty for a prisoner's escape; the innocent suffering for the righteous (Wesley); he withdraws to Caesarea in shame, baffled.

Herod 'highly displeased' (thymomachon, hot quarrel) with Tyre and Sidon, who, dependent on his country for food (cf Ezek 27:17), sue for peace through Blastus — the dispute setting the stage for his public oration.
Calvin: a memorable history showing what end awaits the Church's enemies and how God hates pride — Herod its lively image.

The 'set day' the second day of games at Caesarea for Claudius (told fully by Josephus, Ant. xix); Herod enthroned in a silver-wrought robe dazzling in the morning sun — human grandeur at its zenith before the fall.

The Gentile crowd 'kept on shouting' (imperfect) 'the voice of a god' — pagan emperor-worship flattery to win his favour (matching Josephus); Herod received the honour due to God alone without rebuke.

The angel 'smote him' (epataxen, the verb of v7's gentle touch, here severe) — because he gave not God the glory, accepting divine honour; eaten of worms, like Nebuchadnezzar's pride before a fall (Dan 4:30); confirmed by Josephus.
Calvin: God resists the proud (1 Pet 5:5) — Herod the lively image; the angel who freed Peter now smites the persecutor.
'Grew and multiplied' (imperfect) — the reaction from James's death and Peter's imprisonment; the chief persecutor's death left free scope for the preachers.
Calvin: with the tyrant removed the church was suddenly delivered — the church always outlives her enemies.

Barnabas and Saul return to Antioch, the famine-relief ministry to Jerusalem fulfilled (Acts 11:29-30), taking John Mark (Barnabas's cousin, son of Mary) — stitching the episode back into the gospel's advance toward the first missionary journey.
